RCATitle: Snowflake Putnic latency
RCADescription: Load Surge from ARM Horizontal ScalingAround Oct 16, ARM scaled out, causing a spike in parallel NIC operations (PutNic) for Snowflake subscription. This increased concurrency and contention on NRP frontends.Throttling Feedback LoopLegacy throttling and WorkLimiter policies (TooManyUnfinishedRequests, TooManyRequests) returned 1-second Retry-After, creating retry storms during peak load.Batch queue timeouts (30s) triggered retries on the same request, amplifying contention.
Title: [Snowflake][CRI] Heightened throttling and Service Degradation for Snowflake Subscription - 4575fb04-6859-4781-8948-7f3a92dc06a3 West US 2

RCATitle: CosmosDb Api Call Response Failed in USSouth
RCADescription: Root causeThe issue was caused by RU exhaustion driven by very large documents in the DistributedWorkRequestQueue. During a short burst, ReplaceItemAsync and ReadItemAsync operations against this queue accounted for ~66% of total RU consumption.ReplaceItemAsync averaged ~322 RU per call and peaked at ~1,296 RUHigh retry rates amplified RU pressure, leading to localized throttlingWhy it happenedBatch work‑request documents accumulated significant metadata, inflating per‑operation RU cost. Under peak activity, this resulted in a brief throttling cascade across multiple operation types.MitigationThe system self‑mitigated after sustained healthy samples; usually no manual intervention was required but customer impact should be accessed for each issue.Additional contextSeveral similar, auto‑mitigated incidents were observed in the USSouth region over the following days, all sharing the same RU‑pressure pattern.Please refer to the attached RCA deck (ICM_Incident_Analysis_714605640.pptx) for detailed timelines, metrics, and data breakdowns.Full RCA is here: ​​ICM_Incident_Analysis_714605640.pptx

RCATitle: VTAP Appliance Release Delete ENR Workflow Gaps
RCADescription: As of Jan 2026 in NSM version 22.2, there is a known issue that will block nic instance deletion with VTap if PutENR fails. There is a race condition within the nic instance release code path that causes the nic instance to get stuck waiting forever for a delete signal from VNC via PubSub that will never come.Root Cause: If PutENR fails on the allocation workflow for a NIC with a vtap, the NIC instance will be stuck on deletion workflow. When the NIC is being deleted, NSM attempts to send a DeleteENR for a resource that VNC never created. However, since this resource was never created, VNC will skip publishing the the deleted appliance status back to PubSub. NSM's release code path waits indefinitely for the deleted status from VNC, causing the NIC instance to remain in a pending deletion state and preventing MAC address release.If this error is encountered, the current workaround is to verify that the leaks all had failed PutENR calls, and once the PutENR calls have been validated, manually publish the deleted /appliance/status to PubSub to unblock nic instance deletion.

RCATitle: Cross-partition NRP call timeouts caused NamedLock convoy in southeastasia, failing 1,536 VM operations across 61 subscriptions
RCADescription: NRP cross-partition call timeouts combined with NamedLock convoy caused 1,536 CRP NetworkingInternalOperationError failures across 61 subscriptions in southeastasia on 2025-12-04 10:45–10:55 UTC. AllocateTenantNetworkResources operations make NRP-to-NRP calls via southeastasia.network.azure.com to fetch tenant data from other partitions. When these calls intermittently timed out (30s × up to 14 retries), the calling operations held NamedLocks for up to 600s (background task timeout), blocking all concurrent operations on the same resources. ARM aggregate health is 99.8% — this is NOT an ARM outage. The issue is NRP-internal cross-partition congestion amplified by unbounded retries during lock-holding.

RCATitle: VipManager No Free Vips Available ISE should be user error.
RCADescription: VipManager inventory cache is currently refreshed every 15 minutes. If the customer uses up their vip inventory within those 15 minutes, NRP will not throw a validation exception in the synchronous part, but will instead call RNM in the background task and fail. Because this is a user issue, we can throw a validation exception in the background task to avoid the monitor firing. We can set a separately tuned monitor for frequency of this error to catch regressions and consider refreshing cache more frequently on the partition.

RCATitle: Putnic failures
RCADescription: A load increase starting Dec 3–4 was confirmed across multiple signals: Increase in unique private IP allocations by ~20–25% (from ~22K to ~33K per 5 min).Increase in PutNIC request spikes from ~2500 to ~3500 per 5 minutes (~40% increase).This extra load caused batch manager degradation, not code changes: Subscription lock duration grew from ~500 ms to ~1500 ms.Queue lengths and queued job count increased sharply beginning Dec 3. RCATitle: [Prod_USEast] High Memory Utilization
RCADescription: The reason for high memory usage is due to inefficient memory allocation in preparing the Vnet goal state in GetNetworkResources call. There are two problems:The VnetResource.ToString() method uses several string concatenation to convert the payload to string for emitting to log (serviceExecutionEvents table, and perhaps other places). When there is a huge vnet goal state. this cause excessive allocation in LOH and lead to fragmentation.ConfigurationBlobSerializer.Serialize() method serialize the entire vnet goal state to memory stream (allocating a new memory stream each time), and then converting the memory stream to string. This allocating the entire goal state twice in memory. Again, with large payload, both allocations go into LOH and caused fragmentation.Because LOH is not compacted, fragmentation causes the process to allocate more memory each time, eventhough the allocated objects are relatively short lived. On a Gen4 machine, this eventually crosses the threshold. There is one particular vnet -- 766d15d7-489a-4083-aaa1-a50488962876 owned by subscription 0fc3c32d-cfc3-4105-8d2d-7d0ddfe498fa (External customer), that very frequently call GetNetworkResources and caused this partition to use more memory than others. The reason why the vnet goal state payload is huge is because it has a lot of ServiceTunnelConfig.
Title: [Prod_USEast] High Memory Utilization

RCATitle: [Prod_USEast] High Memory Utilization
RCADescription: The reason for high memory usage is due to inefficient memory allocation in preparing the Vnet goal state in GetNetworkResources call. There are two problems:The VnetResource.ToString() method uses several string concatenation to convert the payload to string for emitting to log (serviceExecutionEvents table, and perhaps other places). When there is a huge vnet goal state. this cause excessive allocation in LOH and lead to fragmentation.ConfigurationBlobSerializer.Serialize() method serialize the entire vnet goal state to memory stream (allocating a new memory stream each time), and then converting the memory stream to string. This allocating the entire goal state twice in memory. Again, with large payload, both allocations go into LOH and caused fragmentation.Because LOH is not compacted, fragmentation causes the process to allocate more memory each time, eventhough the allocated objects are relatively short lived. On a Gen4 machine, this eventually crosses the threshold. There is one particular vnet -- 766d15d7-489a-4083-aaa1-a50488962876 owned by subscription 0fc3c32d-cfc3-4105-8d2d-7d0ddfe498fa (External customer), that very frequently call GetNetworkResources and caused this partition to use more memory than others. The reason why the vnet goal state payload is huge is because it has a lot of ServiceTunnelConfig.
Title: [Prod_USEast2] High Memory Utilization

RCATitle: Incorrect application ID in manifest blocked new subscription Microsoft.Network registrations
RCADescription: The incident was caused by a recent deployment that introduced an incorrect application ID in the manifest file, which blocked registrations of the Microsoft.Network resource provider for new subscriptions in the Mooncake environment. This was a code/configuration change error specific to newly created subscriptions within the last two days, while existing subscriptions were unaffected. The problem manifested as the provider remaining in a 'Registering' state with timeout errors. Multiple rollback attempts faced pipeline and validation challenges before successful restoration to a stable manifest release resolved the issue. Monitoring and queries confirmed the scope of about 60-63 impacted subscriptions, and communications were targeted accordingly. This issue impacted multiple regions in China, as indicated by automated detection models.
Title: [Mooncake] | Network provider Register | Microsoft.Network provider state is Registering all the time

RCATitle: Semaphore deadlock due to inline continuation
RCADescription: NsmConnectionFactory.DisposeClient acquires a semaphore but never releases it. Although fabricConnection.CloseClientChannels() completes successfully, the async inline continuation prevents the subsequent semaphore.Release() from executing. This causes all other threads waiting on the semaphore to be stuck indefinitely, leading to thread starvation.

RCATitle: Excessive Cosmos DB throttling caused by bulk activation of global CVG runners
RCADescription: The incident was caused by a recent migration and bulk activation of dataplane runners (CVGSwiftRegConnRnr and CVGSwiftPERegConnRnr) in a new test tenant, which ran globally in parallel across regions. These runners created large global Connected VNet Groups (CVGs) spanning many regions, causing an exponential increase (approximately O(x^3)) in Cosmos DB Request Units (RU) consumption due to increased document size, cross-region publish status updates, and parallel updates. This spike triggered throttling (429 TooManyRequests errors) and retry storms that further amplified the load, overwhelming Cosmos DB and resulting in widespread failures across at least 56 regions. The majority of failures (91%) were linked to these runners and internal operations rather than affecting customer-facing services. This issue was related to a deployment change (code and configuration around runner migration and activation) rather than external dependency. Logs showed repeated Cosmos DB ReplaceItemAsync 3200 and TooManyRequests errors correlated with runner activity, confirming the root cause.
Title: Global CosmosDB Throttling [Main ticket]

RCATitle: Race condition design defect in Network Service Manager (NSM) caused VM networking failures
RCADescription: The incident was caused by a design defect in the Network Service Manager (NSM), specifically a race condition that led to schema violations for TenantLogicalVip objects. This defect caused failures in control plane network operations and virtual machine networking updates within a specific cluster in the West US2 region. The issue was not related to any recent code deployment or configuration change, but rather an inherent bug in NSM's design. The problem manifested as an increasing number of schema violation exceptions indicating that TenantLogicalVip objects were not properly linked to Allocated IPv4 VIPs. Internal monitoring detected these anomalies, impacting about 380 customer subscriptions over a two-and-a-half-hour window. Initial mitigation attempts using documented troubleshooting guides failed, leading to delays in recovery. The team then applied a specific configuration change (SetFabricSetting OaaS) to bypass the error, which restored normal operations. Continuous monitoring confirmed the effectiveness of this mitigation, and detailed communications were sent to customers. A formal root cause analysis and plans for a permanent fix are underway.
Title: [High Outage Risk - Please use SetFabricSetting OaaS to mitigate ASAP] NMC Exceptions: In object TenantLogicalVip-TenantLogicalVip:n: Schema Violation: TDM: TenantLogicalVip: It must be linked to Allocated IPv4 vip since IPVersion is IPv4. is significantly increasing in uswest2 MWH01PrdApp14

RCATitle: BRAIN detected an unusual trend in SLI "Reliability for Put VM Calls" for Virtual Machines  in West US 2
RCADescription: The incident was triggered by commit latency spikes and lock contention on macmanager/08, leading to lock timeouts across MAC release operations and later degrading MAC allocations required for VM creation in West US 2. The MAC release workflow's single-threaded design and retry logic caused the release pipeline to stall when macmanager/08 became unhealthy, resulting in inventory depletion and VM creation failures. Failovers temporarily improved commit performance, but orphaned locks and gateway communication delays prolonged the bottleneck. The root cause was not a code or configuration change, but an operational bottleneck in the MAC Manager service, exacerbated by high resource utilization and inefficient release logic.

RCATitle: Certificate validation failure due to missing configuration enablement and bug in the fallback logic
RCADescription: The incident was caused by the addition of the CCME certificate to the node, which led 10% AzNM Gateway services to use the new CCME certificate; however, the Partition Manager service was not configured to trust the CCME issuer certificate. This rejection was due to two main issues: (1) a feature flag that retrieves valid certificate issuers via the DSMS GetIssuer library was only enabled in asiaeast Az03, but Az01 and Az02 has the feature flag disabled, and (2) a bug in the legacy failback logic causing issuer validation to fail when the new feature flag was disabled. These 2 configuration and code bug prevented Partition Manager from accepting the new CCME certificate, resulting in failures when processing NRP forcedelete requests, and Update/Delete container requests.

RCATitle: Deadlock in NRP batch manager
RCADescription: The ExecuteJobsWithResourceLockingTransactions contains this code (watch the STEP comments below):public async Taskstring, Exception>> ExecuteJobsWithResourceLockingTransactions(...){ ... IEnumerable tasks = jobs.Select( // STEP 1: The runtime selects job A to execute. async (job) => { // STEP 2: This call increases the count of participants in the barrier. await explicitResourceLockManager.RegisterLockManagerExecution(lockManager).ConfigureAwait(false); ... try { using (ResourceLockingTransaction lockingTransaction = ...) { await job.JobAction(lockingTransaction).ConfigureAwait(false); } // STEP 3: This call decreases the count of participants in the barrier and REPLACES the barrier by another fresh one. // Whenever this throws an exception AND there are additional jobs, the bug is triggered. await explicitResourceLockManager.SignalTransactionSuccessAndAwaitCrossTransactionCommitIfNeeded(lockingTransaction).ConfigureAwait(false); ... } catch (Exception ex) { // STEP 4: This call decreases the count of participants in the NEW barrier, which was NOT incremented by this participant in STEP 1. // This causes the number of participants to be -1 (BTW, there is no sanity check against that). await explicitResourceLockManager.SignalTransactionFailed(lockingTransaction, willRetry: false).ConfigureAwait(false); ... } } ); await Task.WhenAll(tasks).ConfigureAwait(false); ...} After above events, the following happens on the SAME chunk of jobs; the deadlock is on step 7: public async Taskstring, Exception>> ExecuteJobsWithResourceLockingTransactions(...){ ... IEnumerable tasks = jobs.Select( // STEP 5: The runtime selects job B to execute. async (job) => { // STEP 6: This call increases the count of participants from -1 to 0. await explicitResourceLockManager.RegisterLockManagerExecution(lockManager).ConfigureAwait(false); ... try { using (ResourceLockingTransaction lockingTransaction = ...) { await job.JobAction(lockingTransaction).ConfigureAwait(false); } // STEP 7: This call decreases the count of participants in the barrier from 0 to -1, // and then it WAITS FOREVER, because there is no other job in the chunk and // therefore the count never becomes 0 again. await explicitResourceLockManager.SignalTransactionSuccessAndAwaitCrossTransactionCommitIfNeeded(lockingTransaction).ConfigureAwait(false); ... } catch (Exception ex) { await explicitResourceLockManager.SignalTransactionFailed(lockingTransaction, willRetry: false).ConfigureAwait(false); ... } } ); await Task.WhenAll(tasks).ConfigureAwait(false); ...} This bug is uncommon because of these reasons:1. The SignalTransactionSuccessAndAwaitCrossTransactionCommitIfNeeded very rarely throws. It threw this time due to a sequence check failure exception on commit. 2. The runtime typically starts all job tasks before the first one finishes. If SignalTransactionSuccessAndAwaitCrossTransactionCommitIfNeeded throws and there are no jobs left, it doesn't matter if the async barrier becomes negative.It requires two coincidences to make this happen, but when it does, is catastrophic for the region+subscription.

RCATitle: Out of date PA in PubSub caused connectivity issue
RCADescription: The incident was caused by mismatched data between NsmPlus and PubSub. PubSub held out-of-date PA in recentPAList for the PE. The out-of-date PA became invalid recently and caused connectivity issue. NsmPlus lost track on the out-of-date PA after failover. After the failover, resolved PA did not change for a long time. Then NsmPlus did not need to publish again to PubSub to update the recentStablePA. The last published timestamp in PubSub was back to 2024. It indicates the response from DNS has not changed since then.This was a race condition which caused the mismatched data between two infra services. Though the customer has vnet deployed in uswest and useast. But the issue is clearly only in a single region. If the customer can trigger any update on the PE, that will mitigate the issue.

RCATitle: Error during Redirect on Gateway when subscription sharding feature is enabled
RCADescription: In cross-sub Redirect cases when VMSS calls are redirected from compute subscription to networking subscription, FE returns Http Status code 302 with response containing 'Location' header and network subscription id to forward the request to.We enabled a new feature (subscription sharding) in Gateway service on 02/05/26, which had a code bug due to which Gateway service could not correctly fetch the network subscription Id to forward the request to. This is the error in Frontend service, when Gateway did not append correct network sub id: The new code in Gateway, assumed that 'Location' header would be present only in subscription sharding case, which was wrong assumption, and hence incorrectly took the new code path. This new code path had a bug, as when it identified the case is not a valid subscription sharding scenario, it did not set the network subscription Id correctly. Hence, the call got routed incorrectly.

RCATitle: Dangling virtual network due to deleted subscription not properly cleaned in CVG
RCADescription: The incident was caused by a subscription deletion which removed the only virtual network in that subscription. However, the virtual network GUID remained in the CVG because the cleanup workflow failed to fully remove references to the deleted virtual network. This was due to the subscription moving to a different management group hierarchy during the delete flow, causing tracing issues in the global scope and regional goal state mappings. Logs showed numerous CosmosDB 404 NotFound errors when trying to read the removed virtual network, and internal monitoring did not trigger severe alerts due to non-durable log lines and a feature flag for the fix not being enabled. Connectivity was restored after peering was placed between the two virtual networks involved, indicating the root of the connectivity concerns was the dangling reference to the deleted virtual network.

RCATitle: Excessive cross-region load caused by misconfigured data plane runners
RCADescription: The incident was caused by the recent activation of data plane runners that were intended to be deployed only regionally but were inadvertently enabled across all regions. These runners triggered excessive cross-region Cosmos DB calls and global CVG status updates, overwhelming the system and causing widespread 429 errors. The root cause is linked to the runner framework's logic that, during static member delete operations, triggered global CVG updates unnecessarily by attempting to remove VNets from global CVGs where they did not exist. This caused repeated cross-region publish work requests to all 55 regions, resulting in throttling and failure in Cosmos DB API calls. This issue originated from a configuration and deployment change involving the data plane runners, as confirmed by log analysis, Kusto queries, and correlation with runner activity that aligned with error metrics and failure rates.

RCATitle: OutOfMemory Exception Caused Session Leak and Commit Failures in Mac Manager 61
RCADescription: The incident was caused by a System.OutOfMemoryException on macmanager/61 in West Europe, which led to leaked IMOS sessions that blocked subsequent commit operations during allocate/free processes. This memory exhaustion caused session conflicts and lock timeouts, resulting in failures for VM allocation and release for approximately 45 subscriptions. There were no recent code or configuration changes before the incident; instead, the issue appeared after the system had been running for several days, indicating an edge case or bug in code handling long-running transactions or large data loads. Logs showed IMOS transaction commit failures with errors indicating that the version store memory was exceeded, causing FabricExceptions. The impacted Mac Manager instance required a manual failover as the automatic failover did not trigger, and mitigation involved isolating the failing instance to restore service. The root cause is thus a code-related memory leak and session handling bug triggered by high memory usage leading to out-of-memory conditions.

RCADescription: I've been debugging some incidents these last few weeks related to how we use the dependency injector via the UnityContainer library. I'm not sure of the best steps moving forward to resolve these incidents. Library - GitHub - unitycontainer/unity at release/4.0.x Related Incidents this has caused (just a few samples) Incident 579123829 : [Merlin] State Machine Failure for Microsoft.Practices.Unity.ResolutionFailedException exceeded threshold occurred in AMS06PrdApp18 Incident 643032049 : [Merlin] State Machine Failure for Microsoft.Practices.Unity.ResolutionFailedException exceeded threshold occurred in MAD20PrdApp05 Incident 655284087 : [Merlin] More than 500 LogicalNicStateMachine stuck in WatcherConnecting state over 3 mins in uscentral DSM14PrdApp28 Two main issues Sometimes unity container will resolve to a stale element despite the register call be invoked Sometimes unity container will not resolve to anything Root cause Able to repro in test, there seems to be some kind of race condition such that unity container doesn't resolve properly when set multiple times

RCATitle: New PubSubProxy feature caused a perf regions
RCADescription: The newly introduced feature of using PubSubProxy to publish to PubSub introduced lock contentions. The publishing logic in PubSubProxy is synchronous. Thus, it held the lock on name records longer than before when publishing was async when using PubSub client directly. A fix is to make the publishing async. New monitors will be created to cover this scenario and related.

RCATitle: Subscription running AKS load-test clusters
RCADescription: Subscription 33922553 is running AKS load-test clusters (xscenarios-loadtest-southus-3 and -6) using Peregrine in ussouth. During rapid VMSS scale-out, AllocateVirtualMachinesOperation fired concurrently for 318+ single-VM tenants, each acquiring an explicit PublicIP lock. This exceeded the 10K/5min threshold at 23:00 UTC. The burst was transient — locks returned to 0 by 23:35 UTC with no NRP partition health impact, no customer impact, and no throttling required.

RCATitle: No Free Mac Address Available in Canada Central at specific slice
RCADescription: List of available MacManagers to allocate MACs is gets updated every 15 minutes in every NRP slice (10 production slices) in every region.Canada Central is a "special" region because contains 21 Available MacManagers, but 4 of them are technically not available given their whole Inventory allocated is marked as InUse and not supposed to be in-rotation to be used to allocate MACs from NRP given their lack of free Inventory (fabric:/rnm/macmanager/06, fabric:/rnm/macmanager/07, fabric:/rnm/macmanager/08, fabric:/rnm/macmanager/09)During one on that process of update in Slice 6, NRP invoked to RNM to get the current MAC Utilization of every MacManager in the region and RNM only returned a single MacManager (fabric:/rnm/macmanager/08) which given its lack of free Inventory started to fail the MAC allocations requests, along with that, given that was the only MacManager returned blocked the workflow to attempt the MAC allocation into others MacManagers given there were not others during this time window.Incident got auto mitigated on the next time the update ran as got the whole list of MacManagers available and allocations succeeded normally again.

RCADescription: This incident was caused by high loads by the subscription. The query below shows ~1.2M throttling exceptions. Open in [ADX Web] [Kusto.Explorer] [Fabric] [cluster('nrp.kusto.windows.net').database('mdsnrp')]let _incidentTime = datetime(2026-03-06 14:29:53);let _startTime = _incidentTime - 1h;let _endTime = _incidentTime + 30m;let _region = "canadacentral";let _operationName = "AllocateVirtualMachinesOperation";let _pid = "14580";let _roleinstance = "NRP_YTO2107.4";let _subscription = "bd1720a4-9e90-4cbf-ba4d-e19030728ab2";QosEtwEvent| where PreciseTimeStamp between (_startTime .. _endTime)| where Region == _region| where SubscriptionId == _subscription| where ErrorCode != ""| summarize count() by ErrorCodeErrorCodecount_TooManyRequests1,116,036RetryableError4,550TooManyUnfinishedRequests92,612InternalServerError47DropGatewayTimeoutRequest290NotFound14AnotherOperationInProgress1The high number of exceptions led to high CPU usage as shown on the dashboard NRP PerfConsequently, there was thread pool exhaustion, which meant there weren't enough threads to execute existing jobs in the Batch Manager at an acceptable rate.

RCATitle: [AzCiM] CCF_Generation_Failure_Critical : Unable to successfully generate and publish the CCF payload to Madari in useast2euap-prod-d
RCADescription: Root cause:NRP 193 was deployed in east canary region.Rollback test was performed to revert this version back to NRP 192. During rollback, one of the steps for pubsub setting match failed because DCMT doesn't have "Region" pubsub endpoint while NRP config has. This issue is fixed in NRP 193 but is not available in 192. (https://msazure.visualstudio.com/One/_git/Networking-nrp/pullrequest/14624729)When the pubsub endpoint setting verification deployment step failed, it was skipped to proceed with next step of deployment, which took the source of pubsub endpoints from Settings_Prod.xml(outdated) and applied it to NRP config. (Above validation step not only validates, but preserves the endpoint info in NRP).This step shouldn't have been skipped as it is risky that pubsub endpoint snapshot can be reverted to an older value based on the build version. Instead, when the step fails, PutDynamicSetting should be called to preserve the settings so that rollback build cannot overwrite it based on settings_prod.xml file.
Title: [AzCiM] CCF_Generation_Failure_Critical : Unable to successfully generate and publish the CCF payload to Madari in useast2euap-prod-d

RCATitle: IBSM lifecycle management bug: InfinibandStateMachines are not recreated when HpcUfmEndpoint transitions from empty (reserved) to non-empty (standard).
RCADescription: Cluster DUB60PrdGPC08 had its HpcUfmEndpoint set to empty for ~6.5 days (March 20 00:21 ? March 26 ~18:01), effectively placing it in "reserved" mode. During this period, a failover at 16:30 UTC on March 26 created 3,368 backend InfinibandStateMachines across 842 containers, all initialized with an empty UFM endpoint.When the endpoint was restored at ~18:01 UTC, the existing IBSMs were NOT recreated or restarted. The current codebase's OnHpcUfmEndpointSettingValueChanged handler only unregisters PKeys but keeps the SM alive in a dysfunctional zombie state. It does not trigger SM recreation when the endpoint transitions from empty to non-empty.This left 376 backend ContainerInterface objects with dysfunctional IBSMs that the Merlin-MissingStateMachine monitor correctly flagged at 21:03 UTC.
Title: Merlin state machine is missing for ContainerInterface object in europenorth DUB60PrdGPC08

RCADescription: This is NOT a service-wide outage. Only one customer's deployment is affected. The 400s and 404s from other customers are expected errors.The NullReferenceException is a code bug in the AVNM SecurityAdminRule PUT handler for API version 2025-05-01. RCATitle: Rnm slowness and excessive throttling
RCADescription: impact chian:The VMSS provisioning flow is: Allocate ? Commit ? Finalize (RNM deploy) ? done. The Finalize stage collapsed:+--------------------------------------------------------------------------+¦ Time ¦ Finalize Avg ¦ Finalize P95 ¦ Finalize Max ¦ Reverts ¦+-----------+----------------+------------------+--------------+-----------¦¦ 20:00 ¦ 306 ms ¦ 102 ms ¦ 29s ¦ 0 ¦+-----------+----------------+------------------+--------------+-----------¦¦ 20:30 ¦ 610 ms ¦ 102 ms ¦ 47s ¦ 14 ¦+-----------+----------------+------------------+--------------+-----------¦¦ 21:00 ¦ 3,204 ms ¦ 4.3s ¦ 105s ¦ 12 ¦+-----------+----------------+------------------+--------------+-----------¦¦ 21:15 ¦ 11,012 ms ¦ 54.9s ¦ 535s ¦ 666 ¦+-----------+----------------+------------------+--------------+-----------¦¦ 21:30 ¦ 147,152 ms ¦ 520.8s ??49× ¦ 635s ¦ 1,437 ¦+-----------+----------------+------------------+--------------+-----------¦¦ 21:45 ¦ 149,953 ms ¦ 554.9s ¦ 632s ¦ 2,128 ¦+-----------+----------------+------------------+--------------+-----------¦¦ 22:30 ¦ 224,425 ms ¦ 594.4s ¦ 708s ¦ 2,627 ¦+--------------------------------------------------------------------------+FinalizeTenantAllocation calls RNM to deploy fabric resources. Once RNM saturated (~21:15), Finalize went from 300ms ? 2–10 minutes. The CRP orchestrator then timesout the overall VM provisioning and issues RevertTenantNetworkAllocation to roll back the successfully-allocated NICs.Corrected Vicious Cycle 2-4K successful allocations/15min ¦ ? FinalizeTenantAllocation ? RNM deploy ¦ RNM overloaded ? Finalize 300ms ? 150,000ms (500×) ? CRP provisioning timeout ? issues Revert ¦ ? Revert ? calls RNM to DELETE ? RNM already overloaded ¦ Each Revert stuck 5 min (PendingDeleteInFabric) ? Reverts consume concurrency slots ? hits 300 ceilingThis timeline matches NRP log, which shows RNM is the sole bottleneck.Open in [ADX Web] [Kusto.Explorer] [Fabric] [cluster('nrp.kusto.windows.net').database('mdsnrp')]QosEtwEvent| where Region == "uscentraleuap"| where TIMESTAMP > ago(1d)| where Success == false| where RoleInstance == "NRP_CDM0301.18"| where ErrorDetails contains "RegionalNetworkManager did not finish deleting" //| where OperationName == "DeleteTenantOperation"| summarize dcount(OperationId) by bin(TIMESTAMP, 5min)| render timechart

RCATitle: AVNM and NSP HTTP 5xx errors caused by non-ASCII characters in resource group names being passed as gRPC metadata headers
RCADescription: Root Cause Analysis (RCA)SummaryAVNM and NSP HTTP 5xx errors caused by non-ASCII characters in resource group names being passed as gRPC metadata headers via the modern grpc-dotnet (Grpc.AspNetCore) listener, which enforces RFC 7540 ASCII-only header validation.TimelineApr 11, 2026: Regression introduced when UseModernGrpcDotNet=True was enabled globally starting from version 9.7.3, switching all MNC services (Gateway, NetworkSecurityPerimeterManager, VnetStateManager, etc.) from legacy Grpc.Core to modern grpc-dotnet (Kestrel HTTP/2).Apr 17, 2026: ICM 780560828 filed. Investigation started.Apr 17, 2026: PFGold hotfix submitted — UseModernGrpcDotNet=False with VersionUntil="9.7.2" applied globally.Apr 17–18, 2026: Hotfix rolled out to canary regions first (useast2euap, uscentraleuap, usstagee, usstagesc, uswestcentral, asiaeast, australiaeast), validated zero failures.Apr 18, 2026 ~00:30 UTC: Hotfix fully deployed to all remaining regions. All MNC service failures (Gateway, NSP, VnetStateManager) dropped to zero globally.Apr 20, 2026: Confirmed 64 regions reading UseModernGrpcDotNet=False, zero ASCII header failures from any MNC service in 3+ days.Root CauseCode Path:Customer creates an Azure resource with a non-ASCII resource group name (e.g., 서버1_group, brügmann-software, 2023年10月17日作成, PeñaColorada-RG).Gateway receives the ARM request and URL-decodes the resource group name into a NetworkManagerId or NetworkSecurityPerimeterId.NMContext / NSPContext constructors add these IDs to a Properties dictionary.BaseContext.EncodeProperties() serializes all Properties into gRPC Metadata headers for inter-service communication (Gateway → backend services).With the modern grpc-dotnet listener, the underlying .NET Http2Connection.WriteHeaderCollection → HPackEncoder.EncodeStringLiteral enforces RFC 7540 compliance and rejects non-ASCII characters in HTTP/2 headers.This throws System.Net.Http.HttpRequestException: Request headers must contain only ASCII characters, returning HTTP 500 to the customer.Why legacy Grpc.Core worked: The legacy C-core gRPC implementation did not enforce ASCII-only validation on metadata headers, allowing non-ASCII bytes to pass through.Affected Customers (10 identified):서버1_group (Korean)brügmann-software (German)2023年10月17日作成 (Japanese)피터_리소스그룹 (Korean)ağ (Turkish)小雨嘀嗒ai (Chinese)PeñaColorada-RG (Spanish)Projekt_Mähren_IT_2.0 (German)Brügmann-Software (German)上課一 (Chinese)Affected Regions (10 regions with active customer impact before hotfix):indiacentral, europewest, koreacentral, useast, japaneast, francec, ussouth, japanwest, useast2euap, uswestcentralMitigationImmediate Hotfix (PFGold config change):Set UseModernGrpcDotNet=False globally in ManagedNetworkController.xml:ApplicationParameter Name="UseModernGrpcDotNet" Value="False" VersionUntil="9.7.2"/>This reverts all MNC services to the legacy Grpc.Core listener with ServerCredentials.Insecure, which does not enforce ASCII header validation. The ApplicationUpgradePolicy has ForceRestart="true", so all service replicas restart and pick up the new flag on deployment.Validation:64 regions confirmed reading UseModernGrpcDotNet=FalseZero ASCII header failures from any MNC service (Gateway, NSP, VnetStateManager, PolicyManager, NotificationManager) across all regions for 3+ daysPatched canary regions (useast2euap, uscentraleuap, asiaeast, australiaeast, usstagee, usstagesc, uswestcentral) showed immediate drop to zero failuresNote on MonSvc: A small trickle of residual failures (~6/hr) was observed from fabric__ManagedNetworkControllerMonSvc — this is the AzCP monitoring sidecar, a separate application not controlled by MNC's UseModernGrpcDotNet flag. These are self-resolving as MonSvc replicas cycle.

RCATitle: [Aegis Reliability][CRP] Observed failure rate increase in Region [uksouth] starting at 2026-04-18 10:20:00 for ["Databricks Serverless"] for CRP operations with ResultCode [NetworkingInternalOperationError] and ExceptionType [nSystem.Threading.Tasks.TaskCanceledException]
RCADescription: This incident (ICM 781950310) is a recurring, known design limitation in the NRP BatchTransactionManager. During a 10-minute window (10:20–10:30 UTC on April 18), CRP's NRP.Validate HTTP calls timed out for 134 Databricks Serverless VM PUT operations in UK South, surfacing as NetworkingInternalOperationError / TaskCanceledException. The NRP side shows no pathological condition — no hot instance, no elevated queue saturation, no deployment change, no partition instability. The regional failure rate spike was modest (+2pp, from 9.5% to 11.5%) and fully self-healed by 10:35 UTC. An identical past incident (ICM 761789305, March 13 2026) was investigated by DRI ericodoom, who confirmed the root cause as "Batch Manager Subscription Locking Impact" — a TransferPreProvisionedMacAllocationsOperation held a subscription-level lock, stalling concurrent AllocateVirtualMachinesOperation requests, which caused NRP.Validate to exceed CRP's timeout. This was classified as "Service - Design Limitation / By Design until Batch Manager is replaced." Recommend mitigating as transient/by-design.

RCATitle: Subscription 4494d41e-7f5f-4323-824f-edaab813d058 (Databricks/Arcadia workload with arcadia-bbc-* resource groups) ran a full-subscription resource enumeration
RCADescription: Subscription 4494d41e-7f5f-4323-824f-edaab813d058 (Databricks/Arcadia workload with arcadia-bbc-* resource groups) ran a full-subscription resource enumeration starting at ~21:00 UTC, issuing ~28-32 parallel GetResourcesOperation calls across 20+ NRP resource types (VpnServerConfiguration, ApplicationSecurityGroup, RouteTable, VpnGateway, ApplicationGateway, VirtualHub, etc.). Each call timed out after 12-20 minutes (avg 784,803ms), holding IOCP threads open for the full duration. With 900 concurrent timeout-pending operations, this exhausted the .NET ThreadPool IOCP threads on NRP_CO201.1 (climbing from 457 to 808). Combined with sustained CPU at 85-91%, this triggered the Compound Alert (iocp+CPU). At peak (21:20), this single subscription consumed 95.7% of the role instance's total thread time (550.8M of 575.7M ms). PID 13724 recycled at ~21:32, clearing the accumulated threads and restoring normal operation (current AvgDuration=141ms).

RCADescription: SpotLoadTestBVT automation in subscription c44f0821-1d75-475e-9a7d-41a55be84138 overwhelmed AllocateTenantNetworkResourcesOperation in South Central US STG, generating 53,088 operations (~95.3% of all traffic) with only 2.83% success rate. All other 22 subscriptions maintained 100% success, confirming this is isolated test noise with no customer impact. This is the 4th+ recurrence of this exact pattern in this region.

RCATitle: GetResources  trafic load
RCADescription: Subscription 0b5b4487-3e5d-4208-9b05-1a0db2ee9810 issued parallel GetResources enumerations across 20+ NRP resource types (NSG, NIC, VPN, ER, LB, PLS, RouteTable, NatGateway, VirtualHub, etc.), each timing out after 20-43 minutes while holding IOCP completion port threads. With 15+ concurrent operations holding threads for 20-40 minutes each, the 386 available IOCP threads were rapidly consumed, triggering the ThreadPool_Exhausted compound alert (IOCP=386 + CPU=84.5%). The same subscription also generated heavy VM lifecycle churn (~960 Allocate/Disconnect/Delete cycles in 1 hour, Avg 12-15s each), further contributing to thread pressure. This is a recurring pattern -- the identical failure mode hit NRP_MWZ204.7 just 16 hours earlier (ICM 784437185).

RCATitle: [[NRP] GatewayService Sev2] NRP_ [West US 2][WindowsFabricPartitionDown_West US 2] is unhealthy.
RCADescription: The alert is the retrospective symptom of a short, region-wide SF partition-health flap (03:15–03:25Z) that was triggered when a pre-existing, chronic 46-minute DeleteTenantOperation retry loop on a single stuck VM coincided with an IOCP cold-worker injection on NRP_MWZ204.8 at 03:05Z. The partition primary's tenant-chain lock held long enough for the region-wide SF PartitionDown metric to tick up 157 events, then self-recovered by 03:30Z.Root cause (specific, verified): VM VMGPAPSYNGS4 (tenant id 154D27F6-F8E0-442B-9694-4E29343C923F) has been stuck in CRP-retrying DELETE for at least 25 hours (35 confirmed attempts, 04-22 12:16Z → 04-23 13:55Z, still ongoing at snapshot). Every attempt fails at ~46 min with NetworkingInternalOperationError, CRP fires the next retry. The hang is waiting on fabric service-instance d7464960-1690-479d-9aa8-927319811dfa.0 cleanup on cluster uswest2-prod-a (V1 Basic-SLB path) — NRP has already finished its own object cleanup; the outstanding item is the fabric ack that never comes back.Classification: transient partition-health flap under a chronic, sub-specific stuck-delete retry loop.
Title: [[NRP] GatewayService Sev2] NRP_ [West US 2][WindowsFabricPartitionDown_West US 2] is unhealthy.

RCATitle: Region-wide NRP frontend queue saturation caused VMSS API timeout failures
RCADescription: The incident was caused by saturation of the Network Resource Provider (NRP) BatchTransactionManager queues across all 30 frontend instances in the westus2 region. This saturation led to gateway drops after exceeding the 45-second timeout threshold, causing numerous TaskCanceledException errors on ValidateVMScaleSet API calls. The saturation was a capacity degradation unrelated to traffic spikes or recent deployments. This queue overload caused delays and timeouts processing VM scale set operations, resulting in elevated API failure rates impacting 220+ subscriptions. Logs showed 112,410 queue timeout errors and 10,890 gateway drop events during the incident. The total request volume steadily declined during the incident, confirming this was an internal processing bottleneck rather than external load. No code deployment or configuration changes were noted that contributed to the saturation, and the issue self-healed as queues naturally drained by 00:35 UTC.

RCATitle: Region-wide allocation bursts overwhelm NIC state machine pipeline
RCADescription: The incident was caused by repeated massive allocation and deallocation bursts driven by an external CRP/platform-level event in the East US 2 region. This sustained allocation churn overwhelmed the Merlin NICInstanceStateMachine pipeline across more than 50 clusters, including BN9PrdApp20, leading to state machines stuck in various states such as ProgrammingNic. NSM infrastructure and components (primary, IMOS, PubSub) remained healthy, with no internal exceptions or publish failures detected. The root cause is pipeline capacity saturation due to external allocation churn rather than internal component failures or code/configuration changes. NICs published configuration successfully but did not receive downstream ProgrammingCompletedEvents, likely due to overload conditions. This event correlated with a BRAIN SLI anomaly in VM Allocation Reliability (ICM 784760759) and a region-wide VM allocation issue.

RCATitle: Errors creating VMSS with Vnet Injection V2
RCADescription: There was a new requirement to support coexistence of Sharedvnet and Vnetinjectionv1 on same subscription. Until nrp 192 release, the onboarding of sharedvnet is done with enabling enablesharedvnet feature flag alone, hence if a customer wants to do vnetinjectionv1 deployment with same subscription, it will still go to vnetinjection v2 path as request is identical for uniform vmss.The change was made as part of 192 and enabled till pilot in 193. In order to support it, setting injectionmode property to "v2" is enforced from 193 until pilot region for uniform sharedvnet vmss.We are trying to proactively mitigate the customer subscription by enabling feature flag (AllowSharedVnetWithoutInjectionModeV2). code reference:https://msazure.visualstudio.com/One/_git/Networking-nrp?path=/src/sources/Frontend/FrontendContract/Internal/VMScaleSet.cs&version=GBmaster&line=2513&lineEnd=2526&lineStartColumn=12&lineEndColumn=28&lineStyle=plain&_a=contentsbefore we enable everywhere we are gonna mitigate all subscriptions. Below is the safelySafeFly (R2D) - Request #201036

RCATitle: [[NRP] GatewayService Sev2] NRP_ [West US][WindowsFabricPartitionDown_West US] is unhealthy.
RCADescription: A single extremely large subscription (580a84fa) with 10.3M entities generated a massive bursty KVS read load starting at 05:30 UTC, peaking at 1,725 MB per 15 minutes, 100x its normal baseline. Sustained ~1.4 GB/15min load from 05:45-06:15 exhausted resources on machine 10.0.7.41, causing cascading failures and partition unavailability across all co-located services. CommitHangedCritical errors confirmed database pressure, and QoS success rates collapsed to near zero. Multiple gateways and partitions were impacted due to this noisy neighbor effect. No evidence of process crashes or fabric reconfiguration was found. No deployment or configuration changes caused the incident.
Title: [[NRP] GatewayService Sev2] NRP_ [West US][WindowsFabricPartitionDown_West US] is unhealthy.

RCATitle: NRP gateway does not limit outbound connections to NRP frontend, leading to resource exhaustion
RCADescription: GW→FE Request Path — No Circuit Breaker or Self-ThrottlingThe GW request-forwarding path has no mechanism to limit outbound calls to an unhealthy FE partition. When FE becomes unreachable, every inbound request enters an unbounded retry loop that runs until a 50-second global timeout expires. There is no circuit breaker, no concurrency cap on outbound calls, and no back-pressure signal to callers.1. Exception handler — retry until global timeoutGatewayServiceRoutingController.cs — catch block (line 1026) + ShouldThrowException (line 1403)catch (Exception ex) { // No circuit breaker trip. No error-rate tracking. // Just increment a failure counter for alerting (not for throttling). nrpServiceNameToPartitionMapping[nrpServiceName].IncrementFailureCount(); if (this.ShouldThrowException(trackOperationTime, retryCount, ex, ...)) { throw; // Only exits when elapsed ≥ 50s timeout } } // ShouldThrowException logic: private bool ShouldThrowException(...) { // Exits ONLY when elapsed time exceeds the 50-second global timeout. // There is no max-retry-count limit. if (!this.ShouldRetryServiceCall(timeoutForAllRetries, elapsed, retryCount, ex, ...)) return true; if (sleepInterval + elapsed >= timeoutForAllRetries) return true; return false; // Keep retrying } 2. New HTTP connection per request (no client caching)HttpClientWrapper.cs — SendAsync (line 34)public async TaskHttpResponseMessage> SendAsync(...) { Http2Handler http2Handler = new Http2Handler(); if (CommonConfigurationSettings.EnableHttpClientCache) // ← disabled in production { // Would reuse a cached HttpClient per type (GET/PUT/Admin) client = this.nrpGetClient; // or nrpPutClient } if (client == null) // ← always true when cache is disabled { // Creates a NEW HttpClient (and thus a new TCP connection) for every call. client = HttpClientFactory.Create(http2Handler); } try { return await client.SendAsync(requestMessage, ct); } finally { if (newHttpClientInitialized) client.Dispose(); // Connection is closed after single use } } 3. ConsequenceWhen an FE partition becomes unreachable:Inbound rate: 500 req/s (constant from ARM/CRP) Per-request: retries for 50s, each attempt waits for connection timeout Concurrent: 500 × 50 = 25,000 pending outbound calls within 50s Thread pool: saturated at ~1,000 threads New connections: 1 per retry attempt (no HTTP client reuse) Recovery: never (new requests keep entering the same loop)Main evidence (local reproduction):

NRP failure impacted VM and Network
management operations in UK South
